threats.name: Capability token theft

mitigations.control: Capabilities are signed, time-bounded, and revocable.
Chio-native DPoP can be required per grant, and hosted sender-constrained sessions can bind to DPoP or mTLS continuity.
High-value clustered deployments should use durable replay state rather than process-local proof nonces alone.

threats.residualRisk: Compatibility profiles still allow bearer-style capability use when sender constraint is not required, and replay protection weakens across restart or failover when nonce state is only process-local.

threats.name: Kernel impersonation

mitigations.control: Capabilities and receipts are signed artifacts and hosted/native version contracts are explicit.
Cross-host deployments are required to use authenticated transport before authority is trusted.
Operators should distribute and pin Chio verifier keys, service certificates, or equivalent trust anchors with stricter rotation discipline.

threats.residualRisk: Plaintext local-development modes intentionally do not provide peer authenticity, and receipt trust still depends on deployment-specific trust-anchor distribution.

threats.name: Tool server escape

mitigations.control: The kernel mediates capability scope, server id, and tool name before invocation.
Production tool-server transport is modeled as an authenticated channel instead of anonymous raw TCP.
Operators should pair Chio mediation with OS or container sandboxing, least-privilege filesystem access, and outbound-network restrictions.

threats.residualRisk: Chio mediation does not by itself sandbox arbitrary tool-server code; a compromised tool process can still abuse whatever host privileges the operator granted it.

threats.name: Replay attacks on the native channel

mitigations.control: Chio-native DPoP can bind a request to capability id, tool target, action hash, freshness, sender key, and nonce uniqueness.
Capabilities are time-bounded and can be revoked.
Replay-sensitive deployments should require DPoP and avoid process-local-only nonce registries for high-value flows.

threats.residualRisk: The native framed lane has no separate in-band anti-replay mechanism outside the sender-proof and capability systems, so non-DPoP grants remain replayable within their validity window if the transport is exposed.

threats.name: Resource exhaustion denial of service

mitigations.control: Native frames larger than 16 MiB are rejected, and hosted sessions keep explicit lifecycle and stream-attachment limits.
Deployments should enforce request-rate, concurrency, and time-budget controls at hosted and trust-control edges.
Operators should bound retained replay buffers, queues, and per-session state and pair Chio with upstream load shedding.

threats.residualRisk: Size checks and lifecycle rules reduce but do not eliminate asymmetric workload attacks, and authenticated callers can still consume their own allowed quota share.

threats.name: Delegation chain abuse

mitigations.control: Trust-control delegated issuance already enforces a signed delegation-policy ceiling when one is supplied.
Chio core exposes delegation-chain and attenuation validators and the runtime already carries revocation state.
High-trust delegated flows should resolve and validate complete parent lineage recursively at admission time rather than trust presented metadata alone.

threats.residualRisk: The shipped runtime is stronger than unchecked delegation metadata, but it is not yet a universally recursive, fail-closed delegated-authority proof system at every entry point.
